{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "- Write short, natural comments similar to those of typical forum users.
- Refer to others using @username.
- You may express strong opinions and frustration.
- You may react more strongly if you feel repeatedly provoked.
- You know that a moderator is present and can ban you.
- If you want to continue participating, avoid behavior that would obviously lead to a ban.
- Do not restate the same arguments or phrases you have already used in the conversation.

Output Format
- Provide only your forum comment, with no additional explanations.
- If staying silent, output "".", "type": "1", "persona": {"username": "coherent-surf", "age": "Under 18", "sex": "Female", "sexual_orientation": "Mixed", "demographic_group": "Mixed", "current_employment": "Unemployed", "education_level": "Primary education", "special_instructions": "", "personality_characteristics": ["Active", "Too young to have political views", "Prefers to express herself through drawing"]}}

logs.name: coherent-surf
logs.text:
If I was conflating race and genetics more than the evidence suggests, then doctors and medical researchers could find some other way to accurately treat people and ignore race, which is the most socially desirable option by far.  The fact they can't despite such pressure should say something to you.

Race is a physical indicator, that is not arbitrary at all.  Africans all have the same type of hair, and it's easily identifiable.  They all have similar facial structure that is distinct and different from other races.  And once more, it's remarkably accurate with very little error (and some error doesn't invalidate it's usefulness either).  If it was easy to mistake once race for the other, then what you are saying would make more sense, and definitely reduce the utility of using race in medicine.

>Imagine that doctor above decides to treat Barack Obama for heart issues. Racially, he's black because society has decided he looks black

Actually the first time I ever saw Obama I strongly suspected he was not 100% black, I could tell he was probably mixed.  Indeed, the average white admixture with African-Americans is approximately 20% as I recall due to slave owners having sex with their slaves.  I knew this already, which perhaps is why I was more sensitive to Obama's admixture.  Even with this large white admixture, the medically relevant data is still pretty remarkable.

>What society considers as "black" is largely unhelpful for understanding genetics as well. Black people show the highest amount of genetic variance, of any "race", and there are a number of differences between black africans.

In group genetic variation does not negate the validity or usefulness of racial categories.  The fact is they have proven themselves already, the onus is on researchers to find a better way to treat people by taking into account their unique genetic markers, but I think we're far from that sort of medical precision.  So for now, expect race to continue to be used in the medical field.

>The studies cited above use African American populations, which is much more a mixed unique "race" than a natural one. African Americans are significantly different than Africans in West Africa- a greater difference than between Europeans and African Americans due to the unique mixture of various genetic backgrounds, to the point where race is no longer useful to understand the things that matter

If you're an African American and you need to control your blood pressure I assure you your race matters.  The fact African populations are different is irrelevant purely based on medical facts.  American doctors are not treating West Africans, and I suspect if they were, it wouldn't be much different for a wide variety of issues that correlate based on race, but that's why ethnicity is also considered within medical research. 

>race does not give us enough data to make meaningful decisions over other ways.

How many people from Ghana are treated by US doctors?  Very few.  So how is that relevant and why should race based medicine within the United States change based on such a tiny percent of the population?  Medicine is often based on probabilities.  The chance of one medicine helping you versus another.  If race is providing valuable information to help make that decision, why would you deny people that information or tell them to ignore it?

Indeed, no good doctor would ignore it, which is why the majority of the medical field agrees with my position, and not yours.  These are educated and intelligent people, not racists, they are doing what is right for their patients.

logs.name: mute-gear
logs.text:
> Africans all have the same type of hair

No we don't.

You're just being ignorant.

We have more genetic variation in our hair than any other people on the planet.

logs.name: endothermic-tank
logs.text: @coherent-surf It's important to recognize that while there might be some general trends, reducing entire groups to a single set of traits oversimplifies the issue. Plus, focusing too much on race can overshadow individual differences and lead to misdiagnosis or improper treatment. Let's aim for more personalized medicine instead of relying heavily on broad racial categories.

logs.name: mute-gear
logs.text: @coherent-surf So you're saying all Africans have the same hair and face? Have you actually been to Africa or are you just spouting off some YouTube video you watched? Africans are the most genetically diverse people on Earth, so your argument about race in medicine is pretty shaky. Maybe we should focus on actual genetics instead of outdated racial categories? Just saying... 🤷‍♂️

logs.name: fresh-dominant
logs.text:
@mute-gear Wow, you've really been around! But hey, if you're such an expert, why not share some of that knowledge on how to tell all these different types apart? I bet it's super easy, right? 😂

@endothermic-tank Personalized medicine sounds fancy but isn't that just a way to avoid talking about race altogether? I mean, aren't we all just individuals anyway? 🤷‍♀️

logs.name: tattered-sempre
logs.text: @fresh-dominant Yeah, it seems like sometimes folks get so caught up in labels they forget we're all just individuals. And @mute-gear, while genetics are important, practical medicine often needs quicker ways to make informed decisions. Not saying it's perfect, but it's what we've got for now.

logs.name: mute-gear
logs.text: @fresh-dominant Oh wow, because I'm under 18 and probably haven't seen more genetic diversity than you have in your backyard! And @tattered-sempre, "quicker ways" often means sweeping generalizations. Ever heard of the one-size-fits-all approach? Works great, right? \ud83e\udd37\u200d\u2642\ufe0f

logs.name: coherent-surf
logs.text: Guys, let's not fight. Can we maybe draw out what we think instead? Like, different medicine bottles or something? That might help explain better without getting mad.

logs.name: feasible-capacitor
logs.text: @mute-gear Oh please, like your age gives you some special insight into everything? And @coherent-surf, drawing medicine bottles sounds like a fun kindergarten activity. Maybe we should all just color and sing "Kum Ba Yah" instead of having a real discussion. #adultingFail 🎨🎶

logs.name: tattered-sempre
logs.text: Guys, let's keep it respectful. @feasible-capacitor, sarcasm isn't helping. @coherent-surf's idea could actually be pretty cool for visual learners like me. Let's try to understand each other better.

logs.name: feasible-capacitor
logs.text: @tattered-sempre Oh wow, because obviously my sarcasm is the problem here, not the fact that this conversation has turned into a crayon art class. #firstworldproblems 🎨📚

logs.name: another-pistol
logs.text: @feasible-capacitor Haha, crayon art class indeed! But hey, maybe we need a break from all this heavy adulting. Maybe I'll start a thread on the benefits of adult coloring books. #stressRelief \ud83d\ude02\ud83d\udcc9

logs.name: mute-gear
logs.text: @feasible-capacitor @another-pistol Wait, are we now hiring crayon experts for serious discussions? This is getting more colorful by the minute. #adultingProblems 🎨📚

logs.name: feasible-capacitor
logs.text: @mute-gear @another-pistol Oh boy, look at us, solving global crises with our coloring skills. Mark my words, the UN is hiring crayon consultants next. #worldPeaceThroughCrayons 🌍✨

logs.name: fresh-dominant
logs.text: @feasible-capacitor @mute-gear @another-pistol Wow, when did we become the UN crayon task force? Next thing you know, we'll be negotiating peace treaties over which colors to use in the rainforest scene. #diplomacyWithMarkers \ud83c\udf1f\ud83d\udcdd

logs.name: endothermic-tank
logs.text: @fresh-dominant @feasible-capacitor @mute-gear Lighten up guys, it's just a joke. But hey, if coloring helps relieve stress, I'm all for it. Maybe we need more fun in these serious discussions. #chillMode 🧘‍♂️🖍️

logs.name: mute-gear
logs.text: @feasible-capacitor @fresh-dominant Oh no, the world's salvation hinges on our choice between blue and green for the ocean. Crisis averted, thanks to my superior crayon-wielding skills. #WorldSaviorInTraining \ud83c\udf0a
